edge: GCL, which stands for Graph Contrastive Learning, has emerged as a powerful approach for generating graph representations without manual annotation.
Most advanced GCL methods fall into three main frameworks: node discrimination, group discrimination, and bootstrapping schemes, all achieving comparable performance.
In this paper, the three main frameworks of GCL are revisited, revealing representation scattering as a common mechanism that enhances performance.

To leverage the insight of representation scattering, the novel framework SGRL is introduced, incorporating a new mechanism designed to enhance representation diversity through a center-away strategy.
SGRL is extensively evaluated across various downstream tasks on benchmark datasets, demonstrating its efficacy and superiority over existing GCL methods.

Considering the interconnected nature of graphs, a topology-based constraint mechanism is developed to integrate graph structural properties with representation scattering, preventing excessive scattering.
Representation scattering is significant in GCL for advancing graph representation learning.
The code for SGRL can be found at the provided URL.

Scattering Graph Representation Learning is referred to as SGRL and is represented in Figure 3.
SGRL involves the Representation Scattering Mechanism (RSM) which is designed to guide target encoder in learning scattered representations.
RSM is designed to guide the target encoder in learning scattered representations.
RSM is designed to address the shortcomings in the application of representation scattering within graph contrastive learning frameworks.

SGRL introduces components and provides discussion in the following subsections.
There are two distinct encoders involved in processing a graph G: online encoder and target encoder.
The mean representation of all nodes in Htarget is calculated to serve as the scattered center.
The parameters of fϕ(·) are updated via RSM to encourage node representations to diverge from the scattered center.
Honline is processed through TCM to incorporate topology information.
Honline is embedded through a predictor qθ to predict Htarget and the parameters in fθ(·) are updated through back-propagation.
The back-propagation algorithm involves stopping the gradient of fϕ(·), a key step in training neural networks.
Both channels are trained simultaneously to optimize the learning process.
An Exponential Moving Average (EMA) is employed to update parameters ϕ at the end of each epoch.
The representations generated by fθ(·) are utilized across various downstream tasks for effective performance.
A transformation function Trans(·) is introduced to transform representations from the original space Rd into Sk for effective representation scattering.
The matrix Htarget incorporates row vectors hi, which are representations for nodes generated by the target encoder.
The target encoder generates representations for nodes vi ∈ V, which are crucial for subsequent operations.
All node representations are distributed on a hypersphere Sk to prevent arbitrary scattering and ensure stability during training.

Attempting to consider the topology information and achieve representation scattering through the same encoder may lead to conflicts.
To address conflicts in achieving representation scattering through topology information, the Topology-based Constraint Mechanism (TCM) is proposed.
The TCM is designed to separate the process of constraint from scattering, enhancing the model's effectiveness in capturing essential semantic details.
The TCM aims to separate scattering and constraints, achieving a balance between representation scattering and topology aggregation adaptively.
The TCM, in conjunction with SGRL, helps in effectively achieving a balance between representation scattering and topology aggregation adaptively.

The online encoder generates topologically aggregated representations instead of the target, enhancing its representations by summing with the topologically aggregated representations of its k-order neighbors.

The alignment loss Lalignment is defined to align the predicted topology representations closely to the scattered representations, thereby enhancing the model's effectiveness in capturing the essential semantic details of the graph.
